speaker: interviewer
text: Into the light now, back-- sorry. That's good, just hold it there. All right, if you ID it, now.

speaker: interviewer
text: The date is January 9th, 1995. The survivor is Mr. George Meles. The interviewer is Rosanne Krusner. We're in Toronto, Canada, and the language is English. Life.

speaker: interviewee
text: From the very beginning?

speaker: interviewer
text: From the very-- well, not from Moses or from Abraham.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: From the very beginning. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And camera-- actually, if you pop into the left a bit. Just come a little bit left in the chair, that's it. That's good. OK, and camera is set. You forget about me now, and just--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please introduce yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my name is George Meles. It's spelled M-E-L-E-S, and originally it was S and zed. We dropped the zed in Canada, because people didn't know what to do with the zed. So my name is just plain George Meles. I was born in-- on April 17, on an Ea-- in 1922. It was an Easter Monday, the weather was very nice, sunny. It was about 11:30 in the morning. And my father was enjoying a football match at the same time, and people were giving him the good or bad news that he has a son born.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: A son was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in a-- in a maternity ward of a hospital. Actually, in those days, there were separate hospitals just for childbirth. And when I grew older, my association with my father was just about the same. He was very busy with his business and didn't have much time to spend with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What city were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Ujpest, which is a suburb of Budapest-- now, it belongs to Budapest. And I was raised mostly by my mother, even though my father came home every day. But as-- as I went to school, later, he was still sleeping in the morning when I was going to school at 8 o'clock. I had to be in at school, [INAUDIBLE]. And by the time I came home, it was 1:00, 1:30 in the afternoon, my father was not there anymore. And he went, after his business, to enjoy himself in-- in the coffee house. And by the time he came home in the night, around 1 o'clock, I was already asleep.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there was an opportunity to see him only on Sunday. Actually, I could have seen him other times, because he was sleeping. But the time was limited to Sunday, when he then came home to eat lunch at 12:30, until 1 o'clock. And after he spent another hour to have a conversation with me-- which I really appreciated.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he was a good man. He was providing everything what we needed, what my mother needed. And we cannot complain that we were neglected, financially.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Emanuel, and he came from a family of eight, I guess. My grandfather was born in 1848-- he lived until 1937, and he died at the age of 89. My-- my-- my grandfather was born, also, in Hungary. So was his father. His father's name was [? Pinkus, ?] and [? Pinkus, ?] his grandfather was called Gabriel. Now Gabriel, at the turn of the last century-- not 1700 to 1800, was a very curious name for-- for a Jew. They came to Hungary because they were living in Moravia. Moravia is a part of the Czech Republ-- Republic, and the capital is [? Brn, ?] or Brno. And the reason they left Moravia is because the Jews had no right to marry. Only the oldest son could marry in Moravia, and the rest of the boys couldn't get married, which wasn't very good because most people want to get married and establish a family. But the Moravians didn't want the Jews to-- to-- to have too many children, because too many Jews were no good in-- at that time, as it is no good in most of the countries today. So they were limited, and they came to Hungary. Hungary-- now it was the same country, it was a Austro-Hungarian-- Austro-Hungarian country of the combination of Hungary and Austria. And there was no problem to go from one part of the country into the other, so that was no problem to go and establish yourself in Hungary. The Hungarians were welcoming all sorts of people at that time, because Hungary was under the Turkish rule for about 150 years, and most of the population has disappeared. So actually, the middle part of Hungary-- the flatland-- was uninhabited, and there were all sorts of people-- mostly [INAUDIBLE], the Germans, and Slovaks from Slovakia, who were established in Hungary, where they were living in certain villages. And the villages were speaking only-- the people in the villages-- were speaking only German. Or in the other village, they were speaking only Slovaks. So they weren't learning to speak Hungarian. On the other hand, when the Jews came to Hungary, they were willing to learn Hun-- Hungarian, and they were very successful. During the second half of the 19th century, they were mostly businessmen, and doctors, and lawyers, and they were poets and authors and they were writing in the newspapers. And they were very, very successful-- more not [INAUDIBLE] came during or after the second World War, to Hollywood, were producing many successful movies-- like one of them is and many others afterward. Now eventually, during the-- after the first World War, between the two World Wars, the Hungarians realized that the Jews are taking over the [? literary ?] life of Hungary. And they just didn't like it. And the Jews were oppressed. First they had the numerous clauses, which meant about 3% of the Jews could enter university. And then later on, after '38, there was a [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], which meant that no Jew could enter the university.

speaker: interviewee
text: So by the time I finished the high school, which was an academic high school in 1940, the gates of the universities have closed for me. And so were every other opportunity. So I was there ready to go, and I just couldn't go anywhere. Can you tell me about your mother, and her side of the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother-- my mother's family were also-- were born and raised in Hungary. And my grandfather--

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eliash, [? which one ?] is Elias. And they were-- great-- my great-grandfather was born in 19-- 1840. And he-- he was always remembering that 1840-- 1848, there was a Hungarian freedom fight. The Hungarians wanted to be inde-- independent from the Hapsburgs. He was standing under a palm tree, while he-- while the-- the Austrians and the Hungarians were shooting at each other. That's the oldest incident I remember mentioned in the family. Now, he had four-- four sons. And the only-- my grandfather, who was born in 19-- 1870, was the only one who had children. The other three, even though one has married, the others, too, haven't-- they didn't produce any offspring.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my mother was born in 1899, and her mother died before she was-- she wasn't even one year old. What was your mother's first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Berta. She was raised by her grandparents, and the grandparents were not that young. And later on, her mother-- her grandmother also died, and then she was raised by her father, and her father's second wife. And it was a real miserable life, the way she described it. My mother's life was similar to those pictures of movies, which ones were made early in this century, in the-- in the the 18-- 1910, 1920, where everything was very, very sad. And eventually my mother married my father. And this didn't work out very well, either, for her. And then in 1944, she was taken to a concentration camp, and she has pe-- perished there. So I cannot-- I cannot remember if I have read, or I have heard, of anybody whose life was more miserable than my mother's. One-- so much from-- my mother had a brother from the-- from his father's first wife, and he was in a forced labor camp in 1944, and he died there. The second wife of my grandfather-- who, by the way, never came to see me. I wasn't a very popular kid. My father was not at home when I-- wasn't at the hospital when I was born. My grandfather didn't see me until I was nine-- nine years old, by-- because at that time, he was immigrating to-- to New York, and my mother wanted her father to see his only grandchild at that time. So I was one of those kids, as they say, only his mother liked at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were very close with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: You were close with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was close to my mother, but my mother was a kind of a woman who was more or less a little bit cold. Not because she didn't love me. She loved me very much, but she couldn't show it because she never received love from anybody. Like, with absolutely nobody. So therefore, we respected each other. My mother helped me with my studies in the school. She gave all her effort to help me in any way. But by the way, my mother was going for-- for elementary school, another-- like four years in elementary, and then four years in a kind of a commercial school, where she was learning German and some commercial-- commercial, whatever. My father went to academic high school. And he-- he was living-- he was born and finished elementary school in a village. And he seemingly was a very bright boy, and they sent him to an academic high school after the four years of elementary school. Unfortunately, his parents were not very well-to-do, and he was living in a-- at a family who has taken him in during the year. And he had to go and eat, each and every day, at another Jewish family. So it wasn't much fun, as he related to me. And after four years, he just said, he has had it. As a young boy, imagine 10 years old boy, who is living at-- at a strange Jewish family. And on top of this, if he wanted to eat, he had to go to a different Jewish family for seven days of the week. Now, this wasn't really very encouraging for him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of Jewish home did you have? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a very what we called it a [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] is a kind of a Jewish family, which you would call a reformed Jewish family. My mother lit a candle on Fridays-- not necessarily on each and every Friday, whenever she remembered. But I was brought up as-- as a Jew, as a good Jew. My grandfather was a very religious Jew, who was celebrating the coming and the going of the Sabbath.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my grandmother had a wig-- [INAUDIBLE] I thought it's ridiculous. If she had to shave her head, why to wear a wig? This is just a circum-- it has [? circum-going ?] of-- of the law, of the Jewish law. So if you shave your head, then you should have your ha-- hair shaved. But of course, at that time, I didn't think of it. Were you educated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in the school in Hungary, each and every religion was supposed to be taught in the school. And the Jews were having the rabbi, or a good substitute for a rabbi, who was teaching in school. I had a rabbi of our city teaching me when I was 17 and 18, the last two years of the high school. And that's a sad story about this rabbi, who was a very talented man. He has written about four or five books on religious affairs. And in 1944, he had a chance to escape from deportation because there were Jewish organizations, a Zionist organization, who helped certain people who were either Zionist or committed or talented people, they thought are worth saving. And he refused. And he said, I'm not going to leave my flock in trouble. If they are going to be deported, I'm going to be deported. Soon after, when they have arrived-- when he has arrived to Auschwitz, he got into a kind of a unit where they were putting the dead persons' clothing in order-- like the shoes are in one part, the clothing into [INAUDIBLE], children's were in the other [INAUDIBLE]-- children and old people were gassed immediately. And one day, he has found his 12-years-old son's shoe, and this broke him. And I am not-- I'm not surprised. And then he perished pretty soon after.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name? The name of the rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Danish Freedman. I think Danish in English is Daniel. I think. And he was Dr. Freedman. And he was well respected, even though he was only in his 40s at that time. And I think he was-- well, he was the rabbi when I was [? bar mitzvahed ?] at-- in '35, and-- 1935. And he was conducting himself very respectably. And as I told you before, he was a knowledgeable person.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the relationship like between the Jews and the non-Jews in your city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had no problem. In 19-- now, during the rule of the Hapsburgs, the Hungarian Jews didn't suffer any discrimination. Many of the Hungarian Jews beca-- got baronetsy-- like they were barons. And-- but of course, you had to do something. You had to have either a lot of money, or you had to excel in some kind of a profession.

speaker: interviewer
text: But during the '30s when  you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's before. Well, when the war was over, there was a new ruler coming to Hungary, was elected. His name was-- was Horthy, Miklos-- that's Nicholas-- Horthy. And it's very interesting that the Hungarians selected a person who couldn't speak Hungarian. He spoke German. And his wife did speak Hungarian. And when there was supposed to be-- he had to appear and make a speech, he didn't make the speech. His wife was making the speech. But he wasn't even present, because they didn't want the Hungarians to know that their ruler didn't speak Hungarian. Well, which wasn't surprising. The British had George I, George II, who most probably spoke only German. So it's nothing to be ashamed of. But he was just lucky. When he came to rule in 1920s, 1921s, the Jews were-- were in trouble, because they were killing Jews, the ones who were traveling on trains. They were taken off the train, and they just-- they were just executed by this new ruling class. This one has discontinued after 1922, because the Hungarians wanted to participate in the Western world, and it just didn't go very well at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in the time of your father, for instance, when he was-- what did he do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his occupation, your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a merchant [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he experienced any--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I just don't know. We didn't meet that many times, that he could tell. He mentioned to me that he was joining the war, the first World War, in 1914, and in 1915 he was in-- in a combat-- hand-to-hand combat-- and he was wounded in-- in the neck. And this wound healed, but I could see where he was pierced by a Russian soldier. And at that same time, he became a prisoner of war, and he was taken to Siberia. And he was there in a camp, and seemingly, the camp didn't have any fences, because it was several thousand miles away from Hungary. So nobody was afraid that he was going to run away. It was about 100, 150 kilometers from the Mongolian border. It was south of the Baikal Lake-- you know, where Irkutsk [INAUDIBLE] was. It was another lake, which they call it the [? Barlhas-- ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he--

speaker: interviewee
text: --which was a smaller lake.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry, was he treated differently in the army as a Jew, or as a captive in--

speaker: interviewee
text: No he wasn't. He was just a Hungarian prisoner of war. There was no discrimination in those days. And a prisoner of war is a prisoner. He was lucky, because he got acquainted with a farmer in Siberia whose son was a prisoner of war in Hungary. And according to my father, he said, I'm going to treat you well in the hope that where my son is a prisoner of war, he's treated-- will be treated or is treated by a Hungarian [? farmer ?] there someplace. So he would-- There was an interesting incident. My father didn't know how to cook, but he got hold of some rice. And he was having a big pot, and filled-- filled it with rice, and put some-- and as you know, the rice is epanding real rapidly. And he says, at least a dozen people were eating rice on that day, because the rice was just boiling over, over, over, and people came with plates. Now this was in Siberia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience any anti-Semitism when you were in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in the school, I couldn't say that. We had professors in the school who were-- were rightists, like extreme rightists. But in the school, they didn't practice it. They-- I was well-liked by most of the teachers-- we called them professors, even though it wasn't university-- because I was a quiet boy. So if I wasn't very talented, at least I-- at least I behaved. So this helped me a heck of a lot, because people like students who are not causing any trouble. So-- so I got through high school. I got-- I was supposed to take some kind of a job, between 1940 when I finished high school and between 1943 when I was taken to a forced labor camp. And I was in a shoe manufacturing business, and I have been there for about a year, year and a half. Now, in Hungary, there were two anti-Jewish laws. One was in 1938, right after when Pius XII, who was called at that time Pacelli, who was celebrating Eucharistic Congress in Budapest. The minute he stepped out of the country, the first anti-Jewish law was enacted. And at that time, my wife's father was exempted because he had silver and bronze decoration in the first World War. A year later, there was another anti-Jewish law, which one restricted the number of Jews who could be employed. There was only so many percentage who could be employed. And since my father was fairly well-to-do, I didn't need the money, so where I was employed, I was told, you don't need the money, let another Jew-- poor Jew have your job. And I asked myself-- Now, then I was employed by private individuals. All I had to do, to have a piece of paper to show in case they catch me on the street of Budapest, that I'm employed. If-- if they catch-- they caught me, and I could show that I'm employed, they let me go. If I couldn't show that I'm employed, then I would have been taken into the concentration camp much earlier.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did life--

speaker: interviewer
text: About two minutes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did life particularly change for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the worse?

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you feel there was danger?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I was in a concen-- in as a forced laborer, then by that time, I was just with Jews. And Jews--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1943, October. And I was taken to Transylvania to the very end of the country, and the name of the place was Bereck-- it's B as in Bob, E-R-E-C-K. And there, we were employed by digging ditches. Like, we had to produce so much-- so much dirt, or sand, or whatnot a day. And if you couldn't do it, then we had to stay out later in the evening. And if you went out to-- and when we went home too late on that day, we didn't get any supper. So that's-- that's the story of the employment.
